content.text: Inference engines execute forward passes efficiently—llama.cpp for broad CPU/GPU support, vLLM for high-throughput GPU serving with PagedAttention, TensorRT-LLM for NVIDIA optimization. Agent platforms pick engines based on concurrency, latency targets, and ops complexity.
What it is
Runtime that loads weights, batches requests, manages KV cache, and exposes HTTP/gRPC APIs. Ollama embeds llama.cpp; production clusters often deploy vLLM or TGI behind a load balancer.
Why it matters
The same Llama weights perform differently across engines—vLLM improves throughput for many parallel agent sessions; llama.cpp excels at single-user local dev.
Comparison
llama.cpp
— GGUF, CPU+GPU, Ollama backend, edge-friendly
vLLM
— continuous batching, OpenAI server mode, multi-GPU
TGI
— Hugging Face stack, enterprise integrations

# vLLM OpenAI-compatible server (example)
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
  --port 8000

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="EMPTY")
print(client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Ping"}],
).choices[0].message.content)
When to use it
llama.cpp/Ollama for dev and edge; vLLM when serving dozens of agent workers from one GPU pool; managed cloud inference when ops team is small.
Best practices
Benchmark tokens/sec and p99 latency with representative agent prompts
Enable request logging and Prometheus metrics on servers
Version engine + model + quant together in deployment manifests
Key takeaways
Inference engine choice affects throughput and latency
llama.cpp/Ollama for local; vLLM for scaled GPU serving
OpenAI-compatible servers minimize agent code changes
Benchmark with real multi-turn agent workloads
